fragments.content: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS characterized by inflammation, demyelination, and neurodegeneration. Clinical courses: relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS, 85% at onset) with discrete attacks followed by remission; secondary progressive MS (SPMS) with gradual worsening after initial RRMS; primary progressive MS (PPMS, 15%) with steady progression from onset. Pathophysiology involves T-cell mediated attack on myelin oligodendrocytes, blood-brain barrier disruption, and formation of periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord plaques. McDonald criteria (2017 revision) require dissemination in space (≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 characteristic CNS regions) and time (simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up). CSF oligoclonal bands support diagnosis. Progressive disease involves microglial activation and compartmentalized inflammation behind closed blood-brain barrier.

MS treatment includes acute relapse management and disease-modifying therapies (DMTs). Acute relapse: high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day × 3-5 days) or oral equivalent; plasma exchange for steroid-refractory cases. DMT selection based on disease activity, progression risk, and safety: (1) Injectable platforms (interferon-beta, glatiramer acetate)—modest efficacy, excellent safety, first-line for mild disease. (2) Oral agents: dimethyl fumarate (monitor lymphocytes, PML risk), teriflunomide (hepatotoxicity, teratogenic), fingolimod/siponimod/ozanimod (S1P modulators, bradycardia risk, macular edema, PML). (3) Infusions: natalizumab (high efficacy, PML risk—requires JC virus monitoring), ocrelizumab (anti-CD20, PPMS approved, infection risk), alemtuzumab (anti-CD52, highest efficacy but secondary autoimmunity risk), cladribine (immune reconstitution approach). Treatment strategy: escalation (start moderate, escalate if breakthrough) vs induction (start high-efficacy early for aggressive disease). Symptomatic management: baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity, gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain, amantadine/modafinil for fatigue, anticholinergics for bladder dysfunction.

MS diagnosis uses 2017 McDonald Criteria requiring dissemination in space (DIS) and time (DIT). DIS: ≥1 T2-hyperintense lesion in ≥2 of 4 CNS regions: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord. DIT demonstrated by: (1) simultaneous presence of gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, OR (2) new T2 or enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, OR (3) positive CSF oligoclonal bands (allows DIT diagnosis at first scan). MRI protocol: brain and entire spine with and without gadolinium. Typical findings: ovoid periventricular lesions perpendicular to ventricles (Dawson's fingers), juxtacortical U-fiber involvement, infratentorial lesions, short-segment spinal cord lesions (<3 vertebral segments). CSF: oligoclonal IgG bands present in >90% (not specific but supports diagnosis). Evoked potentials (visual, somatosensory, motor) detect subclinical pathway involvement. Differential diagnosis includes NMOSD (AQP4-IgG positive, longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis), MOGAD, vasculitis, sarcoidosis, B12 deficiency, Lyme disease.
